planting.note: Direct-sow seeds or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to average soil in full sun in spring; jacaranda requires several years of establishment before blooming — trees grown from seed may take 7–10 years; grafted trees bloom much sooner. Frost kills the tops in zone 9 but established trees typically resprout from the base.

directions: Direct-sow seeds or plant container specimens in well-drained, sandy to average soil in full sun; jacaranda is a South American deciduous tree that is one of the most spectacular flowering trees in the world — in spring, before or as the leaves emerge, the entire tree becomes covered in masses of large, tubular, lavender-blue to violet-purple flowers arranged in billowing panicles that transform the canopy into a breathtaking cloud of blue-purple. The flowering display lasts 6–8 weeks and the fallen flowers carpet the ground beneath the tree in a second violet display. The finely divided, fern-like, bright-green, doubly-pinnate leaves are attractive through the growing season. Jacaranda is the definitive 'purple flowering tree' of warm-climate landscapes worldwide — seen lining streets in Los Angeles, Johannesburg, Sydney, and throughout zone 9–11 in the southern United States. It is frost-sensitive, with established trees tolerating brief dips to approximately 25°F in zone 9 — tops may be killed by hard freezes, but established root systems often resprout. Requires a dry season to flower most reliably. An iconic specimen tree for zone 9–11 gardens.

planting.note: Direct-sow seeds or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun in spring; golden trumpet tree is drought-tolerant once established and actually flowers most abundantly after a period of moisture stress followed by rain — a brief dry season before the main wet season triggers the most prolific flowering displays.

directions: Direct-sow seeds or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; golden trumpet tree (formerly Tabebuia chrysotricha, now Handroanthus chrysotrichus) is one of the most spectacular flowering trees of the Brazilian cerrado and is widely planted throughout tropical and subtropical landscapes for its extraordinary spring flower display — the entire tree becomes covered in large, vivid, deep-golden-yellow to butter-yellow, funnel-shaped, trumpet-form flowers while the branches are leafless or just beginning to leaf out, creating a breathtaking all-yellow display against the blue sky. The flowering season typically lasts only 2–3 weeks but is of extraordinary intensity. The national tree of Brazil is a related species (Handroanthus serratifolius, Tabebuia serratifolia — the Yellow Ipê), and the genus includes pink, purple, magenta, and lavender flowering species as well. Hardy to approximately 28°F in zone 9 with established trees often recovering from frost damage. Excellent as a street tree and garden specimen in zone 9–12. Also known as the golde Ipê.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun in spring; flame vine blooms most abundantly in winter and early spring following a dry rest period — maintain moderately dry soil from October through December to trigger the most prolific flowering display.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; flame vine (golden shower vine, orange trumpet vine) is a South American evergreen tropical vine of the bignonia family that creates one of the most spectacular mass-flowering displays of any vine in the tropical and subtropical garden — in winter and early spring (December–March in the Gulf South), the entire vine, which can cover a fence, wall, or support structure for 30 feet or more, becomes completely blanketed from end to end with thousands of long, tubular, brilliantly vivid orange-red to flame-orange flowers arranged in dense, rounded clusters, transforming the entire structure into a cascade of blazing orange that is visible from great distances. The winter flowering season is particularly valuable in zone 9–12 winter gardens when few other vines or shrubs produce color. It is a vigorous grower that requires strong support and substantial space. In zone 9 it may be killed back by hard freezes but typically resprouts vigorously from established roots. Excellent on large arbors, fences, trellises, and covering walls where its spectacular winter display can be fully appreciated.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; cape honeysuckle is extremely drought-tolerant once established and blooms most freely in lean, dry soil in full sun — rich, moist conditions produce rank foliage with reduced flowering.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; cape honeysuckle is a South African evergreen scrambling shrub-vine that is one of the most reliable and versatile hummingbird plants for Gulf Coast and southwestern zone 9–11 gardens, producing clusters of long, tubular, brilliant scarlet-orange to orange-red, honeysuckle-like flowers in flushes from fall through spring — at the opposite end of the warm season from most summer-blooming hummingbird plants — when migrating and wintering hummingbirds most need reliable nectar sources. It grows as a large, lax, sprawling shrub or a climbing vine if given support, and can be trained against walls, over fences, or left to sprawl as a groundcover on banks. Tolerates partial shade (blooming acceptably in open tree canopy), drought, heat, poor soil, and alkaline conditions once established. In zone 9 it may freeze back in hard winters but recovers quickly from established roots. 'Aurea' (clear-yellow flowers, more refined in habit) is an attractive alternative to the standard orange form. Excellent as a slope cover, espaliered vine, and sprawling hedge.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Argentine trumpet vine tolerates partial shade more successfully than most Bignonia family vines and produces acceptable flower display in the filtered light of open tree canopy — one of the better shade-tolerant flowering vines for zone 8–11.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; Argentine trumpet vine (lavender trumpet vine) is a South American evergreen or semi-evergreen vine of the bignonia family that produces clusters of large, flared, trumpet-shaped, soft-lavender to pale-lilac and white flowers with violet-purple striping and veining on the throat in spring (April–May) and intermittently through the year, creating a refined and graceful floral display above its dense, glossy, dark-green, tendril-climbing foliage. The soft, delicate lavender-lilac color and the large, attractive, trumpet flower form give it a more elegant and less brash appearance than orange or red trumpet vines. It is notably more shade-tolerant than most Bignoniaceae vines and can bloom acceptably in partial-shade positions — a valuable attribute for the shadier garden where most large flowering vines fail. Hardy to approximately 20°F in zone 8, it is one of the more cold-tolerant of the tropical trumpet vine family. Excellent trained on arbors, fences, and large trellises in zone 8–11. May be invasive in some warm, moist climates — monitor for naturalization near natural areas.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center
